figures.name: Awen (The Flowing Spirit)
figures.gloss: The divine inspiration and poetic frenzy central to modern Celtic spirituality. Symbolized by three converging rays of light, it represents the vital spark that animates art, magic, and the living earth, granting the practitioner vision and voice.
figures.facet: Unlike purely transcendent enlightenment, Awen is radically immanent and artistic—it does not detach the seeker from the physical world, but plunges them into ecstatic co-creation with nature.
figures.provenance: Medieval Welsh poetry (Taliesin); elevated to a central theological concept in the 18th century by Iolo Morganwg; formalized in modern praxis by OBOD.

figures.name: The Wheel of the Year
figures.gloss: The eight-fold seasonal cycle combining solstices, equinoxes, and four Celtic fire festivals (Samhain, Imbolc, Beltane, Lughnasadh). It anchors modern Druidic ritual time, mirroring the agricultural and solar cycles.
figures.facet: It is a deliberate modern synthesis that elegantly weaves previously distinct insular and continental agricultural timelines into a single, cohesive liturgical rhythm for contemporary practice.
figures.provenance: Synthesized in the mid-20th century by Ross Nichols (founder of OBOD) and Gerald Gardner (founder of Wicca).

figures.name: The Sacred Center (Fire, Well, and Tree)
figures.gloss: The tripartite cosmic axis established in ritual space, comprising the Sacred Fire (celestial and transformative), the Sacred Well (underworld and ancestral), and the Sacred Tree (the connective pillar).
figures.facet: It intentionally requires no literal, permanent temple; any physical grove or backyard can be temporarily elevated into the cosmic center through the ritual invocation of these three elemental poles.
figures.provenance: Core to ADF (Ár nDraíocht Féin) liturgy, developed by Isaac Bonewits based on comparative Indo-European scholarship.

figures.name: The Gatekeeper
figures.gloss: The liminal deity or spirit—often Manannán mac Lir, Cernunnos, or a localized cross-roads figure—invoked to open the portals between the mundane world and the Otherworld during ritual.
figures.facet: They are rarely viewed as adversarial or chaotic tricksters, but rather as pragmatic mediators whose explicit permission and key-turning are functionally required for safe human-deity communion.
figures.provenance: Synthesized from Celtic mythology and broader Indo-European structural rituals in modern Druid orders to formalize the boundary-crossing phase of liturgy.

figures.name: The Three Kindreds
figures.gloss: The overarching community of spirits addressed in ritual: the Shining Ones (Deities), the Mighty Dead (Ancestors), and the Noble Spirits (Nature and Land wights).
figures.facet: It purposefully flattens the hierarchy of traditional polytheism, placing local land spirits, beloved dead, and cosmic gods into a shared, reciprocal ecosystem of offering and blessing.
figures.provenance: Central theological framework of ADF formulated in the 1980s, now broadly adapted across modern eco-paganism.

figures.name: The Three Grades (Bard, Ovate, Druid)
figures.gloss: The three-tiered initiatory pathway of the modern Druid. Bards master memory and song; Ovates master divination, healing, and tree-lore; Druids master ritual, philosophy, and integration.
figures.facet: Instead of representing rigidly inherited castes or social classes, these are reimagined as cumulative, accessible stages of personal psychological and spiritual development, democratizing ancient elite functions.
figures.provenance: Classical accounts of Celtic society (Strabo, Caesar) reinterpreted by Iolo Morganwg as a spiritual curriculum, later standardized by OBOD.

figures.name: The Earth Mother
figures.gloss: The foundational maternal deity of the land and soil. She is typically invoked first in ritual as the ground of all being, the ultimate provider of the resources necessary for sacrifice and sustaining life.
figures.facet: She is addressed not merely as an abstract mythic creator, but as the literal, localized biosphere the practitioner stands upon, fusing ancient mythology with modern ecological consciousness.
figures.provenance: Syncretized from figures like Danu and Jörd; codified in ADF and OBOD rituals as the grounding step of the liturgical order.

figures.name: Cernunnos (The Horned God)
figures.gloss: The antlered god of the wild, animals, wealth, and the deep forest. He embodies the untamed, fertile, and instinctual aspects of the natural world and vibrant masculine energy.
figures.facet: He represents a vision of power entirely divorced from conquest or rulership, focusing instead on fierce stewardship, wildness, and the protective vitality of the woodland ecosystem.
figures.provenance: Historic Gallo-Roman iconography (Gundestrup Cauldron, Pillar of the Boatmen) widely adopted and expanded by mid-20th-century paganism.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The Awen is the vital, flowing inspiration that powers the magical and artistic workings of the three druidic grades.
Intra-tradition: The Gatekeeper is invoked specifically to activate the Sacred Center, turning mundane symbols into true cosmic portals.
Intra-tradition: They form a complementary dyad of the settled, foundational Earth (Mother) and the untamed, deep forest vitality (Cernunnos).
Intra-tradition: A modern structural synthesis where the Sacred Center anchors the spatial axis of ritual, while the Wheel of the Year anchors the temporal axis.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an internal, immaterial fire or spark of divine consciousness that elevates human awareness above the mundane.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as an animating, pervasive spiritual energy or song that empowers individuals to enact change and commune with nature.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Druidry directly imports and invokes the Celtic figure of Danu, elevating her as the supreme Earth Mother archetype in contemporary liturgy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are invoked directly as the literal, immediate biosphere and living soil, grounding ritual in ecological reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Druidic Sacred Tree functionally mirrors Yggdrasil as the central cosmic pillar uniting the upper, middle, and lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern ADF Druidry explicitly modeled its Sacred Fire rituals on Vedic Agni, utilizing comparative Indo-European scholarship to reconstruct a functional hearth cult.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as the essential liminal mediators who must be honored first to open the spiritual roads and permit human-divine communication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shared structural role where a specific liminal deity serves as the cosmic linguist and key-holder to the Otherworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions elevate the active, propitiated dead (ancestors) to equal ritual importance alongside major deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Parallel emphasis on establishing reciprocal, offering-based relationships with the revered ancestors for community guidance and protection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specifically mirroring Shiva in his aspect as Pashupati (Lord of Animals), both are horned or animal-associated lords representing untamed male power in the wilderness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both operate as the sovereign masters of the deep forest, granting or withholding the bounty of the wild to those who approach with respect.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize time as a cyclical, perpetually turning wheel rather than a linear progression, governing the spiritual rhythms of the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose structural parallel in utilizing sequential, deeply symbolic initiatory processes to transmit deep cultural memory and spiritual authority across generations.

proposedArchetypes.name: Divine Inspiration
proposedArchetypes.gloss: A flowing, ecstatic energy or breath from the divine that grants humanity artistic genius, prophetic sight, or magical ability.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Crucial for mapping concepts like Awen, the Greek Muses, or Vedic Vāc, where spiritual power is transmitted specifically through poetic, musical, or prophetic frenzy rather than just static law.
